title: DHS Biodetection Improvement Act
plain_summary: This law tells the Department of Homeland Security to look at how it has worked with special government science labs to find dangerous germs that could harm people. The department must make a plan for how it will work with these labs in the future to get better at detecting these harmful germs. They have to tell Congress about their plan within six months and then give an update report one year later. This will help keep Americans safer by improving how the government finds dangerous biological threats.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
subjects: Drug safety, medical device, and laboratory regulation

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the DHS Biodetection Improvement Act.

sections.title: Section 2 DHS Utilization of Department of Energy National Laboratories and Sites for Certain Biodetection Research and Development Relating to the Missions of the DHS
sections.text:
The head of the Department of Homeland Security must look at how his department has worked with Department of Energy national laboratories and research sites. This review should focus on research and development work that helps the Department of Homeland Security do its job. The review must follow the rules in section three hundred nine of the Homeland Security Act of two thousand two.

Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the head of the Department of Homeland Security must send a report to two congressional committees. These are the Committee on Homeland Security in the House of Representatives and the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs in the Senate. The report must include the review mentioned above plus a plan for how the Department of Homeland Security will work with Department of Energy laboratories and sites to do biodetection research. Biodetection means finding dangerous germs or biological threats in the environment.

The plan must include four main parts. First, it must identify biodetection technologies that can meet the department's needs. These needs are described in official department documents and studies by the Comptroller General of the United States. One example is a study called the National Re-Assessment of the BioWatch Collector Network to Increase the Fraction of Population Covered from November two thousand twenty one.

Second, the plan must create a buying plan to get these biodetection technologies and give them to existing BioWatch locations. BioWatch is a program that monitors the air for biological threats. This buying plan must follow all federal laws and regulations about government purchases.

Third, the plan must include regular outside reviews to find problems or weak spots in these biodetection technologies. It must also recommend backup plans if the technologies do not work as expected.

Fourth, the plan must help develop clear requirements for future biodetection programs. This work should be done with federal, state, local, and tribal governments, colleges and universities, and private companies.

One year after this law is passed, the head of the Department of Homeland Security must send an update to the same two congressional committees. The update must describe the review and plan, including any problems with putting the plan into action.
